It's like, hey, press a button and you get sales leads in your email, your inbox, your calendar.
That's just not how it works.

And in our opinion, companies just like 99% of companies just can't do that.
So they come to us and they get like, you know, like the three or five X the output for their dollar compared to a regular agency.
And we help them accomplish, you know, goals and we have like the right blend of people, you know, not many agencies.

would say like most of them, we don't teach at all.

based on who they were going after.

And so for me, like, I think about it as like, you know, how do you get a business investor ready, despite their growth goals?
It's like some of my clients, they want to take on.
Venture capital.
And then some of my clients want to continue to bootstrap.
And that's going to be a completely different, you know, journey for them.
But there's like an interim period that sometimes looks the same.
Because whether they're bootstrapping or need VC, they need their numbers and their traction to be at a certain place.
I call it kind of like investor readiness is.
I feel like, I mean, having gone through that, like, sometimes not having any numbers and a story, you know, helps a lot more when you're earlier.
That's why I think it's like so hard to raise money if you've like done five years and you have like some revenue, but not a lot.
Yeah, it's like a lot of weird backwards things with raising venture funding, I find.

starts at nine to 12,000 a month.

think your background as a teacher is super interesting because the community is about that.

we generally split things between delivery and then build, which is kind of vague.

So there's no shortage of things to do.

I think what you said about, you know, building growthx.
and the product, and work beyond work with the existing clients.

That interests me.

I really do.

And, you know,
That's like very broad, but my dad always, my dad's an engineer, and he's invented a lot of different things.
And more recently, like, well, what he does is he designed the rock trencher, subsea rock trencher for fiber optic.
So it's like under the sea on the ocean floor, and then it's like digs through igneous rock in the most efficient way, and then lays the fiber optic deep, and then areas it.
But so that's what he does.

But I always used to come to him as a kid and say, you know, like, hey, dad, like, you should invent this, or you should invent that, you know, and he would always tell me, like, okay, well, how are you going to make it work, Sarah?
And I would say, you just put a button on it, you know?
And so I realized, just through the CD planted in me early is that knowing how things work is really important.
And I think there are a lot of marketers and growth.
And people, maybe less on the growth side, but a lot of marketers and business people who don't really know how things work.
And you can't really make them better if you don't know how they work.

didn't even introduce myself now that think about it.

mean, directionally, we're in this, like, you know, like, I believe knowledge work is kind of broken a little bit the way people work.

were just a small team.

guess that's all I could say at this point.

mean, we're lucky we have the past editor-in-chief of TechCrunch handling the PR side.

mean, that's great.
think, yeah, mean, it's just so kind of very, like in my head, it was just like...

not exactly sure.

actually like that you guys are bootstrapped.

no, I'm I mean, do you have an example of one that you did the positioning for?

Where you can achieve incredible results if you just, you know, are using like Chinese, old Chinese medicine and cupping.

And anyway, so kind of like the drive bar for holistic facials.
Right.
That's really cool.
Yeah.
So that's an example.
It really resonated with the beauty industry.
Yeah.
The outreach.
I mean, I booked her like 75 investor meetings.

Yeah.
That's really cool.

I guess how have you built like your own portfolio of like clients?
Is it just through like 500?

Anyway, so I was brought on and then also they had a, he had a startup arm at the time that I managed all the investor relations for and the growth of the product.

That was a story builder application for filmmakers that allowed filmmakers to collaborate on the story in pre-production, so they would save money in post, which is a huge problem in the filmmaking world.
Doing a lot with a small team, and for him, it was like, okay, we have investment, but we also really need to grow the firm.

And for a while there, we were self-funding, so I was able to work with them to win them the Global Four Seasons contract for their commercial filmmaking, and then that was their biggest contract and record sales for like five, six years after.
So they were traveling around the world doing all the filming for Four Seasons.
And he offered me ownership in the company, 10%, and then I turned it down to start working for myself and took him as my first client.
So for me, it was more like...

I wanted the ability to be working with more people at the time, and then I partnered with a bunch of – I was well-connected in Portland to a lot of the agencies, and so I got clients, a lot of clients, through my agency partnerships, and we won, like, Webby's for some of our work.
But, yeah, that was kind of my first bridge, and then all of my clients in the first phase were through relationships.
And then in the second phase, through relationships as well.

So one of my friends in Bend owned a clean beauty skincare line, like Luxury Clean Beauty, and so I managed growth for her and then also was technically her director of operations, but I kind of was trying to ride the fractional wave.
So I joined quite a few companies as, like, fractional.
Yeah.
Like how you found all these.
Except a few.
I'm you off.
No, that's okay.
